paragraphs.text: When the sons of Laban too began to oppress Jacob, as Laban had oppressed him, and to say of him who had enriched them, that he had become rich from their father’s property; and when Laban himself, who earlier had confessed, “I have learned by experience that the Lord hath blessed me at thy coming” (Gen 30:27), changed toward Jacob both inwardly and outwardly — then God appeared to Jacob and said to him: “Return unto the land of thy father” (Gen 31:3). And Jacob called Rachel and Leah and said to them: “Your father, for whom with all my strength I have served, hath changed my wages ten times… but God did not allow him to do me evil, and all your father’s contrivances have turned upon him himself” (Gen 31:6–7). When he promised to give me speckled sheep as wages, thinking that few would be born, a multitude of speckled sheep were born. And when he promised me spotted sheep, thinking that few would be born thus, all were born spotted. Then Rachel and Leah said to him: “Is there any portion or inheritance for us in our father’s house?… All that he had he hath given to his sons, but us he hath sold… and hath quite devoured also our money. He hath wasted thy strength also in these fourteen years in which thou hast served him for us. Now then, whatsoever the Lord hath said unto thee, do; we are ready to go with thee on the day when God shall send thee” (Gen 31:14–16).

Jacob stole away the mind of Laban, and Rachel his gods (Gen 31:19). And they came to the mountain of Gilead, but Laban “pursued after him… and overtook him… And God appeared… to Laban… in a dream, and said to him: Take heed that thou speak not to Jacob aught of evil, great or small” (Gen 31:23–24). Yet Laban could not hide his anger, and said: “My hand can do you hurt, but the God of thy father forbade me yesterday evening” (Gen 31:29). “Why hast thou stolen my gods?” (Gen 31:30), and my daughters, and fled? Beautiful was Jacob’s love for Rachel, who had loved the God of Jacob. Her father’s idols she despised, because she dishonored them not only by carrying them off as something of little importance and worthless, but also because on the day when her father was searching for them, she sat upon them, having “the manner of women” (Gen 31:35).

Laban was not satisfied. And on the next morning, after the true God had appeared to him in the evening, he demanded his gods. Contrary to what he himself had said before — “Thou hast enriched me, because the Lord hath blessed me at thy coming” — he now says: “The cattle are my cattle, and all that thou seest is mine… Come thou, let us make a covenant… and it shall be for a witness between me and thee” (Gen 31:43–44).

At first they accused one another, and Jacob said: “My affliction and the labor of my hands, and all that has been taken from me, God hath seen and appeared to thee yesterday evening” (Gen 31:42). But Laban said: “The cattle are my cattle… and all that thou seest is mine.” But afterward they said: “Let us leave aside all that has been until now.” “And Jacob took a stone, and set it up for a pillar… and all brought a stone, and they made a great heap” (Gen 31:45–46). This heap, raised by many, was to bear witness, as if by the mouths of many, that a covenant was made before many. “And Jacob called the heap the heap of witness” (Gen 31:47) — that is, those who heaped this heap are witnesses that both Laban and Jacob promise not to alter anything in the covenant they have made beside this heap. But that it might be known that this heap was needed only in witness of the covenant, which from this time on they would not break, it is said: “Jacob sware by the fear of his father Isaac,” and Laban said: “The God of Abraham and the God of Nahor judge between us” (Gen 31:53).
